steps.title: Fastest path: mark the figure first
steps.content: Count the relevant faces, dimensions, or scale factor before setting up equations. Lengths scale by $k$, areas by $k^2$, and volumes by $k^3$; for boxes and prisms, write the needed face or volume formula directly.

steps.title: Identify what the equation must represent
steps.content: The container starts with 18 mL. Water drips in at a constant rate (one 0.06 mL drop every 3 seconds), so $v$ is a linear function of $t$ with initial value 18 and a positive slope equal to the drip rate.

steps.title: Calculate the drip rate
steps.content: Rate = $\frac{0.06 \text{ mL}}{3 \text{ s}} = 0.02$ mL per second. After $t$ seconds the added volume is $0.02t$ mL, giving $v = 0.02t + 18$.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — $v = 0.02t + 18$: Rate 0.02 mL/s, starting at 18 mL. Matches the setup exactly. ✓
  • Choice B — $v = 0.06t + 18$: Rate 0.06 mL/s would mean a full 0.06 mL drop every second, not every 3 seconds. ✗
  • Choice C — $v = 0.18t + 18$: Rate 0.18 mL/s is 9 times the actual rate. ✗
  • Choice D — $v = 3t$: No initial 18 mL, and the rate is wrong. ✗
That confirms A is correct.
